title: Nonvacuous Loss Bounds with Fast Rates for Neural Networks via Conditional Information Measures

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

NONVACUOUS LOSS BOUNDS WITH FAST RATES FOR - NEURAL NETWORKS VIA CONDITIONAL INFORMA TION MEASURES

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

We present a framework to derive bounds on the test loss of randomized learning algorithms for the case of bounded loss functions. This framework leads to bounds that depend on the conditional information density between the the output hypothesis and the choice of the training set, given a larger set of data samples from which the training set is formed. Furthermore, the bounds pertain to the average test loss as well as to its tail probability, both for the PAC-Bayesian and the single-draw settings. If the conditional information density is bounded uniformly in the size _n_ of the training set, our bounds decay as 1 _/n_ , which is referred to as a fast rate. This is in contrast with the tail bounds involving conditional information measures available in the literature, which have a less benign 1 _/[√] n_ dependence. We demonstrate the usefulness of our tail bounds by showing that they lead to estimates of the test loss achievable with several neural network architectures trained on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST that match the state-of-the-art bounds available in the literature.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

In recent years, there has been a surge of interest in the use of information-theoretic techniques for bounding the loss of learning algorithms. While the first results of this flavor can be traced to the probably approximately correct (PAC)-Bayesian approach (McAllester, 1998; Catoni, 2007) (see also (Guedj, 2019) for a recent review), the connection between loss bounds and classical information-theoretic measures was made explicit in the works of Russo & Zou (2016) and Xu & Raginsky (2017), where bounds on the average population loss were derived in terms of the mutual information between the training data and the output hypothesis. Since then, these average loss bounds have been tightened (Bu et al., 2019; Asadi et al., 2018; Negrea et al., 2019). Furthermore, the information-theoretic framework has also been successfully applied to derive tail probability bounds on the population loss (Bassily et al., 2018; Esposito et al., 2019; Hellstr¨om & Durisi, 2020a).

Of particular relevance to the present paper is the _random-subset setting_ , introduced by Steinke & Zakynthinou (2020) and further studied in (Hellstrom & Durisi, 2020b; Haghifam et al., 2020).¨ In this setting, a random vector _**S**_ is used to select _n_ training samples _**Z**_ ( _**S**_ ) from a larger set _**Z**_[�] of 2 _n_ samples. Then, bounds on the average population loss are derived in terms of the conditional mutual information (CMI) _I_ ( _W_ ; _**S** |_ _**Z**_[�] ) between the chosen hypothesis _W_ and the random vector _**S**_ given the set _**Z**_[�] . The bounds obtained by Xu & Raginsky (2017) depend on the mutual information _I_ ( _W_ ; _**Z**_ ), a quantity that can be unbounded if _W_ reveals too much about the training set _**Z**_ . In contrast, bounds for the random-subset setting are always finite, since _I_ ( _W_ ; _**S** |_ _**Z**_[�] ) is never larger than _n_ bits.

Most information-theoretic population loss bounds mentioned thus far are given by the training loss plus a term with a �IM( _PW_ _**Z**_ ) _/n_ -dependence, where IM( _PW_ _**Z**_ ) denotes an information measure, such as mutual information or maximal leakage (Issa et al., 2020). Assuming that the information measure grows at most polylogarithmically with _n_ , the convergence rate of the population loss to the training loss is _O_[˜] (1 _/[√] n_ ), where the _O_[˜] -notation hides logarithmic factors. This is sometimes referred to as a _slow rate_ . In the context of bounds on the excess risk, defined as the difference between the achieved population loss for a chosen hypothesis _w_ and its infimum over the hypothesis class, it is known that slow rates are optimal for worst-case distributions and hypothesis classes (Talagrand,

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

1994). However, it is also known that under the assumption of realizability (i.e., the existence of a _w_ in the hypothesis class such that the population loss _LP_ _**Z**_ ( _w_ ) = 0) and when the hypothesis class is finite, the dependence on the sample size can be improved to _O_[˜] (1 _/n_ ) (Vapnik, 1998, Chapter 4). This is referred to as a _fast rate_ . Excess risk bounds with fast rates for randomized classifiers have also been derived, under certain additional conditions, for both bounded losses (Van Erven et al., 2015) and unbounded losses (Gr¨unwald & Mehta, 2020).

Notably, Steinke & Zakynthinou (2020, Thm. 2(3)) derive a population loss bound whose dependence on _n_ is _I_ ( _W_ ; _**S** |_ _**Z**_[�] ) _/n_ . The price for this improved dependence is that the training loss that is added to the _n_ -dependent term is multiplied by a constant larger than 1. Furthermore, (Steinke & Zakynthinou, 2020, Thm. 8) shows that if the Vapnik-Chervonenkis (VC) dimension of the hypothesis class is finite, there exists an empirical risk minimizer (ERM) whose CMI grows at most logarithmically with _n_ . This implies that the CMI approach leads to fast-rate bounds in certain scenarios. However, the result in (Steinke & Zakynthinou, 2020, Thm. 2(3)) pertains only to the average population loss: no tail bounds on the population loss are provided. Throughout the paper, we will, with an abuse of terminology, refer to bounds with an _n_ -dependence of the form IM( _PW_ _**Z**_ ) _/n_ as fast-rate bounds. Such bounds are also known as _linear_ bounds (Dziugaite et al., 2020). Note that the _n_ -dependence of the information measure IM( _PW_ _**Z**_ ) has to be at most polylogarithmic for such bounds to actually achieve a fast rate in the usual sense.

An intriguing open problem in statistical learning is to find a theoretical justification for the capability of overparameterized neural networks (NNs) to achieve good generalization performance despite being able to memorize randomly labeled training data sets (Zhang et al., 2017). As a consequence of this behavior, classical population loss bounds that hold uniformly over a given hypothesis class, such as VC bounds, are vacuous when applied to overparameterized NNs. This has stimulated recent efforts aimed at obtaining tighter population loss bounds that are algorithm-dependent or data-dependent.

In the past few years, several studies have shown that promising bounds are attainable by using techniques from the PAC-Bayesian literature (Dziugaite & Roy, 2017; Zhou et al., 2019; Dziugaite et al., 2020). The PAC-Bayesian approach entails using the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence to compare the distribution on the weights of the NN induced by training to some reference distribution. These distributions are referred to as the posterior and the prior, respectively. Recently, Dziugaite et al. (2020) used data-dependent priors to obtain state-of-the-art bounds for LeNet-5 trained on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST. In their approach, the available data is used both for training the network and for choosing the prior. This leads to a bound that is tighter than previously available bounds. Furthermore, the bound can be further improved by minimizing the KL divergence between the posterior and the chosen prior during training. One drawback of the PAC-Bayesian approach is that it applies only to stochastic NNs, whose weights are randomly chosen each time the network is used, and not to deterministic NNs with fixed weights.

Information-theoretic bounds have also been derived for iterative, noisy training algorithms such as stochastic gradient Langevin dynamics (SGLD) (Bu et al., 2019). These bounds lead to nonvacuous estimates of the population loss of overparameterized NNs that are trained using SGLD through the use of data-dependent priors (Negrea et al., 2019). However, these bounds do not apply to deterministic NNs, nor to standard stochastic gradient descent (SGD) training. Furthermore, the bounds pertain to the average population loss, and not to its tails. Although the techniques yielding these estimates can be adapted to the PAC-Bayesian setting, as discussed by Negrea et al. (2019, App. I), the resulting bounds are generally loose.

## 1.1 CONTRIBUTIONS

In this paper, we extend the fast-rate average loss bound by Steinke & Zakynthinou (2020) to the PAC-Bayesian and the single-draw settings. We then use the resulting PAC-Bayesian and single-draw bounds to characterize the test loss of NNs used to classify images from the MNIST and FashionMNIST data sets. The single-draw bounds can be applied to deterministic NNs trained through SGD but with Gaussian noise added to the final weights, whereas the PAC-Bayesian bounds apply only to randomized neural networks, whose weights are drawn from a Gaussian distribution each time the network is used. For the same setup, we also evaluate the slow-rate PAC-Bayesian and single-draw bounds from (Hellstrom & Durisi, 2020b).¨ Our numerical experiments reveal that both the slow-rate

For the case in which training is performed using SGD, the minimum test loss bounds (averaged over 50 runs) for MNIST reported in (Dziugaite et al., 2020, Fig. 4) are approximately 0 _._ 13 for LeNet-5 and 0 _._ 18 for the 600[2] FCNN. These values are similar to our best bounds, which are 0 _._ 15 for LeNet-5 and 0 _._ 19 for the 600[2] FCNN. For LeNet-5 trained on Fashion-MNIST, our tightest bound on the test loss is 0 _._ 35, whereas the corresponding one in (Dziugaite et al., 2020, Fig. 4) is approximately 0 _._ 36. Taking error bars into account, our bounds are not clearly distinguishable from those reported in (Dziugaite et al., 2020, Fig. 4). It is important to mention that significantly tighter bounds are reported in (Dziugaite et al., 2020, Fig. 5) for the case in which the PAC-Bayesian bound considered therein is used as a regularizer during the training process. Such a direct optimization of the bound does not appear to be feasible for the random-subset setting considered in this paper.

Next, we discuss the results presented in Figures 1e–f. As shown in the figure, while our bounds become tighter in the initial phase of training, they lose tightness as training progresses when momentum is used and smaller training errors (on the order of 0 _._ 001) are reached for the deterministic NNs. This is similar to what is noted by Dziugaite et al. (2020, p. 12). Specifically, when the underlying deterministic NN therein is trained to achieve very low errors (or equivalently, is trained for many epochs), the PAC-Bayesian bound they consider becomes loose, and the corresponding stochastic NN has a significantly higher test error than the underlying deterministic NN.

Finally, the difference in behavior of our bounds in Figure 1e and Figure 1f illustrates the role played by the variances _σ_ 1 and _σ_ 2. In Figure 1e, we set the threshold used to determine _σ_ 1 and _σ_ 2 to 0 _._ 05, which leads to small values for _σ_ 1 and _σ_ 2. In Figure 1f, we use a threshold of 0 _._ 15 instead, which allows for larger variances. The results illustrate the intuitive fact that larger variances yield better generalization bounds at the cost of a higher true test error.
Further numerical experiments, in which we study how the bounds evolve as a function of the size of the training set, and how they are affected by randomized labels, are reported in Appendix D.

## 5 CONCLUSION

We have studied information-theoretic bounds on the test loss in the random-subset setting, in which the posterior and the training loss depend on a randomly selected subset of the available data set, and the prior is allowed to depend on the entire data set. In particular, we derived new fast-rate bounds for the PAC-Bayesian and single-draw settings. Provided that the information measures appearing in the bounds scale sublinearly with _n_ , these fast-rate bounds have a better asymptotic dependence on _n_ than the slow-rate PAC-Bayesian and single-draw bounds previously reported in (Hellstrom & Durisi,¨ 2020b), at the price of larger multiplicative constants. We also presented improvements on previously presented bounds on the average loss by using samplewise information measures and disintegration.

Through numerical experiments, we show that our novel fast-rate PAC-Bayesian bound, as well as its slow-rate counterpart, result in test-loss bounds for some overparameterized NNs trained through SGD that essentially match the best available bounds in the literature (Dziugaite et al., 2020). Furthermore, the single-draw counterparts of these bounds, which are as tight as the PAC-Bayesian bounds, are applicable also to deterministic NNs trained through SGD and with Gaussian noise added to the final weights. On the negative side, as illustrated in Fig. 1e, the bounds turn out to be loose when applied to NNs trained to achieve very small training errors. Moreover, the additional experiments described in Appendix D reveal that the bounds overestimate the number of training samples needed to guarantee generalization, and that they become vacuous when randomized labels are introduced.

Still, the results demonstrate the value of the random-subset approach in studying the generalization capabilities of NNs, and show that fast-rate versions of the available information-theoretic bounds can be beneficial in this setting. In particular, the random-subset setting provides a natural way to select data-dependent priors, namely by marginalizing the learning algorithm _PW |_ � _**ZS**_[over] _**[S]**_[,] either exactly or approximately. Such data-dependent priors are a key element in obtaining tight information-theoretic generalization bounds (Dziugaite et al., 2020).

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper extends the information-theoretic framework for generalization bounds, specifically the random-subset setting, to achieve fast-rate (1/n) convergence for both PAC-Bayesian and single-draw settings. The authors apply these bounds to neural networks on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST, demonstrating that they are nonvacuous and competitive with existing PAC-Bayesian benchmarks.

The paper provides a technically sound extension of existing conditional mutual information (CMI) frameworks to achieve fast-rate bounds. The derivation of the exponential inequality in Theorem 1 and its subsequent application to test loss bounds are well-executed. The numerical experiments effectively demonstrate the practical utility of these bounds for overparameterized neural networks.

Overview issues:

- Tightness and Multiplicative Constants: While Theorem 1 and Corollary 1 successfully achieve a 1/n rate, they introduce multiplicative constants (e.g., the factor of 2 in (12) and (13)) in front of the training loss and the information measure. The paper acknowledges this but does not provide a formal analysis of when these constants make the fast-rate bound strictly worse than the corresponding slow-rate bounds (8) and (9). For a reader to understand the practical value of the fast-rate contribution, it is necessary to characterize the regime (in terms of training loss and information measure magnitude) where the 1/n decay outweighs the penalty of these constants. A plot or a simple analytic condition comparing the two bounds would clarify the trade-off significantly.

- Sensitivity to Gaussian Prior/Posterior Assumptions: The numerical experiments in Section 4 rely heavily on the assumption that both the posterior $P_{W|\widetilde{Z}S}$ and the prior $Q_{W|\widetilde{Z}}$ are Gaussians with specific mean and variance parameters. While this makes the KL divergence (15) tractable, it potentially ignores the complex geometry of the weight space in deep neural networks. The paper should address how sensitive these nonvacuous bounds are to the Gaussianity assumption. Specifically, adding a discussion or a small-scale experiment comparing the Gaussian-based bound to a more flexible distribution or acknowledging the potential looseness introduced by this parametric restriction would strengthen the credibility of the results.

- Data-Dependent Prior Selection Procedure: The procedure for selecting the prior $Q_{W|\widetilde{Z}}$ involves training networks on 10 subsets of the data and averaging their weights. This is a heuristic approach to constructing a data-dependent prior that satisfies the theoretical requirements. However, the paper does not rigorously justify why this specific averaging and variance selection procedure is optimal or even consistent with the theoretical framework's assumptions regarding the prior's independence from the specific subset $S$. A clearer explanation of how this procedure avoids circularity in the bound's validity—or a formal statement of the union bound argument used to maintain validity—would ensure the reader that the reported bounds are mathematically sound.

- Limited Discussion on Generalization in Overparameterized Regimes: The abstract and introduction emphasize the mystery of overparameterized neural networks memorizing random labels. However, the paper's conclusion admits that the bounds become vacuous when randomized labels are introduced. This is a significant limitation that should be explored more deeply in the main text rather than relegated to Appendix D. The paper would benefit from a more explicit discussion on why the current information-theoretic measures fail to capture the generalization behavior in the presence of random labels, as this would provide a more complete picture of the framework's current limitations.

- Formal analysis of the crossover point between fast and slow rates: The paper introduces fast-rate bounds that include multiplicative constants, yet it lacks a formal characterization of the regime where these bounds outperform the existing slow-rate alternatives. To demonstrate the bite of the proposed result, the authors should analytically derive the crossover condition in terms of the training loss and the information measure magnitude. Specifically, providing a plot or a table that maps the region of the (Training Loss, Information Measure) space where the fast-rate bound is tighter would clarify when a practitioner should prefer the new method. This would transform the qualitative observation in the experiments into a predictive tool for model selection.

- Empirical validation of the Gaussian prior/posterior restriction: The numerical results rely on a Gaussian assumption for the weight distribution, which is a significant parametric restriction in the context of deep neural networks. To address the potential gap between this assumption and the actual geometry of the posterior, the paper should include a diagnostic experiment. For example, computing the empirical variance of the weights or comparing the Gaussian-based bound against a baseline that uses a more flexible, non-parametric estimate of the KL divergence would quantify the looseness introduced by the Gaussian assumption. This would provide necessary context for whether the reported non-vacuousness is a property of the bound or an artifact of the chosen parametric family.

- Justification of the data-dependent prior construction: The procedure for selecting the prior $Q_{W|	ilde{Z}}$ via averaging weights across subsets is a heuristic that needs more rigorous grounding to ensure the validity of the PAC-Bayesian framework. The paper should explicitly demonstrate that this construction satisfies the independence requirements of the prior $Q$ relative to the training set $Z(S)$. A brief formal proof or a citation to a specific result ensuring that this averaging procedure maintains the validity of the union bound would remove ambiguity regarding the soundness of the reported experimental values.

Recommendation: accept

Revision targets:

- Add a brief analytical or graphical comparison identifying the regime where the fast-rate bound (12) is tighter than the slow-rate bound (8).

- Clarify the union bound argument used to justify the data-dependent prior selection procedure to ensure the validity of the reported bounds.

- Include a more explicit discussion in the main text regarding why the bounds fail for randomized labels, connecting this to the limitations of the CMI measure in capturing the specific inductive biases of deep networks.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
This paper extends conditional mutual information bounds to fast-rate PAC-Bayesian and single-draw settings for randomized learning algorithms. The theoretical derivations are clean and build directly on established random-subset frameworks. Empirical results on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST demonstrate nonvacuous estimates that compete with existing state-of-the-art bounds. However, the experimental validation lacks critical scaling analyses, and several implementation choices introduce heuristic biases that weaken the theoretical guarantees.

The paper makes a solid theoretical contribution by deriving fast-rate bounds in the random-subset setting and extending them to PAC-Bayesian and single-draw frameworks. The empirical evaluation successfully demonstrates nonvacuous estimates on standard benchmarks. However, the practical utility of the bounds is compromised by heuristic hyperparameter tuning and a lack of empirical validation for the claimed $O(1/n)$ decay rates.

Overview issues:

- Absence of sample-size scaling analysis: The paper derives fast-rate bounds with an $O(1/n)$ dependence but never empirically validates this scaling behavior. Section 4 and Figure 1 only vary the number of training epochs on fixed dataset sizes. To substantiate the claim of fast rates, the authors must demonstrate how the bound tightness actually decays as $n$ increases. Without this ablation, the theoretical advantage of the proposed bounds remains unverified in practice. Adding a series of experiments that systematically vary the training set size $n$ while holding model architecture constant would directly test the $1/n$ decay rate.

- Heuristic tuning of posterior variance introduces selection bias: The procedure for selecting the posterior standard deviation $	heta_1$ relies on a threshold matching the empirical training loss of the deterministic network against an average over five stochastic draws (Section 4). This heuristic choice is highly sensitive to the arbitrary threshold values and the finite number of stochastic samples. Such tuning effectively optimizes the bound post-hoc, which compromises the theoretical guarantee and inflates the risk of overfitting the bound to the specific dataset partition. The authors should replace this heuristic with a principled method, such as maximizing the bound over a continuous range of $	heta_1$ values or using a validation set to select the variance. Reporting the resulting bound tightness without post-hoc threshold tuning would restore confidence in the experimental claims.

- Fast-rate bounds degrade in the low-training-loss regime: The theoretical motivation for fast rates centers on the interpolating setting where training loss approaches zero. Contradicting this expectation, Figure 1(e–f) shows that the fast-rate bounds become significantly looser than slow-rate bounds once the network achieves very low training errors. The paper notes this phenomenon but fails to explain why the underlying assumption of bounded conditional information density breaks down when the network memorizes the data. Clarifying this behavior is essential because it limits the practical utility of the proposed bounds for modern overparameterized networks that routinely interpolate. The authors should analyze the growth of the CMI term as training loss decreases and discuss whether regularization or early stopping is required to maintain fast-rate guarantees.

- Prior construction relies on an unvalidated approximation: The prior distribution $Q_{W|	ilde{Z}}$ is constructed by training ten networks on linearly spaced subsets of $	ilde{Z}$ and averaging their final weights (Section 4). This approximation lacks a theoretical justification for how well the averaged weights represent the true marginal posterior $P_{W|	ilde{Z}}$. If the approximation error is large, the KL divergence term in the bounds will be inflated, rendering the estimates loose regardless of the theoretical rate. The authors need to quantify the approximation error introduced by this averaging procedure or provide a rigorous bound on the distance between the constructed prior and the true marginal. Alternatively, they could adopt a more standard data-dependent prior construction used in PAC-Bayes literature.

- Limited empirical scope and lack of overparameterization analysis: The experimental evaluation is restricted to MNIST and Fashion-MNIST with relatively small networks. The literature context highlights that information measures in overparameterized networks often grow nearly linearly with the number of parameters, which can cause fast-rate bounds to become vacuous. The paper does not test the bounds on larger architectures or discuss how the CMI scales with model width and depth. Expanding the evaluation to include wider networks or providing a detailed scaling analysis of the information terms with respect to parameter count would clarify the applicability of the framework to modern deep learning. This analysis is necessary to address the known limitations of conditional information bounds in high-dimensional settings.

- Union bound inflation during hyperparameter optimization: The authors optimize the bound over 27 candidate values for the prior variance $	heta_2$ and apply a union bound to preserve the $1-	heta$ confidence level (Section 4). This search-and-select procedure inherently degrades the bound's tightness and may mask the actual performance of the theoretical result. The stated $95	heta$ confidence level is a lower bound that becomes increasingly conservative as the number of candidates grows. To fairly compare against state-of-the-art bounds, the authors should either fix the hyperparameters via a hold-out validation set or report the bound tightness after correcting for the multiple testing penalty. Transparently accounting for this optimization cost is required for a rigorous empirical assessment.

- Analytical worked example for a parametric model: The theoretical derivations rely on abstract information measures without grounding them in a concrete, fully-specified model. Readers cannot easily calibrate their intuition about how the conditional mutual information scales with sample size or network width from the current text. A worked example using a standard linear regression or a single-layer linear network would let readers verify the O(1/n) decay analytically. Computing the CMI for this simple setting and tracking its growth against n would demonstrate whether the fast-rate assumption holds in practice. This addition would bridge the gap between the abstract theorems and the empirical neural network results.

- Guidance for estimating information measures in high dimensions: The bounds require evaluating conditional mutual information or Kullback-Leibler divergences between the posterior and prior distributions. The authors restrict their experiments to Gaussian approximations to bypass computational hurdles, but they do not discuss how to estimate these quantities for general, non-Gaussian posteriors. In high-dimensional weight spaces, estimating information densities suffers from the curse of dimensionality, which can render the bounds computationally intractable or highly biased. The paper should acknowledge this limitation and provide practical guidance, such as using importance sampling or variational approximations, to estimate the information terms. Without this discussion, the claimed practical relevance of the bounds remains unverified for models beyond the Gaussian assumption.

- Development of the marginalized prior construction claim: The authors claim that the random-subset setting provides a natural way to select data-dependent priors by marginalizing the learning algorithm over the random subset. This claim appears in the abstract and conclusion but is never mathematically developed or explicitly derived. The paper approximates the prior by averaging ten networks trained on different subsets, yet it does not analyze how this empirical average relates to the true marginal distribution P_{W|Z~}. Deriving the explicit form of this marginalized prior for a simple learning rule, or bounding the approximation error of the averaging procedure, would substantiate the theoretical advantage of the framework. Readers will wonder how this marginalization actually improves prior selection compared to standard data-dependent priors.

- Comparison to alternative fast-rate generalization bounds: The paper positions its fast-rate PAC-Bayesian bounds against slow-rate counterparts and a single recent PAC-Bayes study, but it omits a comparison to other established fast-rate frameworks. Bounds based on local Rademacher complexity or the fast-rate results of Grünwald and Mehta (2020) offer different trade-offs between multiplicative constants and sample size dependence. Verifying that the proposed bounds recover known results as special cases, or benchmarking them against these alternative fast-rate methods, would clarify their relative strengths. This comparison is necessary to determine whether the information-theoretic approach offers a distinct advantage over geometric or complexity-based fast-rate techniques.

Recommendation: Major revision. The theoretical claims of fast rates require empirical validation through sample-size scaling, and the experimental methodology contains several heuristic tuning steps that inflate the bound's tightness without rigorous justification. Addressing these gaps is necessary to establish the practical relevance of the proposed framework.

Revision targets:

- Conduct a systematic scaling analysis varying the training set size $n$ to empirically verify the claimed $O(1/n)$ decay rate of the fast bounds.

- Replace the heuristic threshold-based tuning of the posterior variance $	heta_1$ with a principled optimization or validation-based selection, and report the impact on bound tightness.

- Analyze why the fast-rate bounds degrade in the low-training-loss regime and clarify the breakdown of the bounded conditional information density assumption.

- Quantify or bound the approximation error introduced by averaging weights from ten subsets to construct the prior distribution.

- Expand the empirical evaluation to include wider network architectures or provide a detailed discussion on how the conditional information measures scale with model overparameterization.

comments.title: Missing factor of 1/2 in Gaussian KL divergence formula
comments.explanation: Equation (15) omits the standard 1/2 prefactor for the Kullback-Leibler divergence between two multivariate Gaussians. The correct formula is D_{KL} = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \frac{\|\bm{\mu}_1 - \bm{\mu}_2\|^2_2}{\sigma_2^2} + d \left( \frac{\sigma_1^2}{\sigma_2^2} + \log \frac{\sigma_2^2}{\sigma_1^2} - 1 \right) \right]. This omission inflates all reported KL divergence values and the resulting bounds in Figure 1 by a factor of two. Please correct the equation and verify that the numerical results were computed with the proper scaling.

comments.explanation: The symbol \bar{\bm{Z}} appears here without definition and should consistently be \widetilde{\bm{Z}}. More importantly, the expectation operators for the KL divergences are mathematically incorrect. By definition, D(P_{W|\bm{Z}}\,||\,P_{W}) = \mathbb{E}_{P_{W\bm{Z}}}[\imath(W,\bm{Z})], meaning the expectation must be taken over the joint distribution of W and \bm{Z}, not a conditional distribution. The same error appears in the second KL divergence. Rewrite these equations to use the joint distributions P_{W\bm{Z}} and P_{W\widetilde{\bm{Z}}\bm{S}} in the expectation subscripts to match the standard definition of KL divergence.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency for test set indices in Corollary 1
comments.quote: \mathbb{E}_{P_{W|\widetilde{\bm{Z}}\bm{S}}}\big{[}L_{\bm{Z}(\widetilde{\bm{S}})}(W)\big{]}\leq 2\,\mathbb{E}_{P_{W|\widetilde{\bm{Z}}\bm{S}}}\big{[}L_{\bm{Z}(\bm{S})}(W)\big{]}+\frac{3\left(D(P_{W|\widetilde{\bm{Z}}\bm{S}}\,||\,Q_{W|\widetilde{\bm{Z}}})+\log\frac{1}{\delta}\right)}{n}.$ (12)
comments.explanation: Section 1.2 explicitly defines \bar{\bm{S}} as the modulo-2 complement of the subset indices \bm{S}, and this notation is used consistently in Theorem 1 and equation (10). Equations (12) and (13) abruptly switch to \widetilde{\bm{S}}, which is undefined in this context. This creates ambiguity about whether the test loss is evaluated on the complement set or a different construct. Replace \widetilde{\bm{S}} with \bar{\bm{S}} in both equations to maintain notational consistency.

comments.title: Sublinear scaling condition is insufficient for faster rates
comments.quote: ngs. Provided that the information measures appearing in the bounds scale sublinearly with $n$, these fast-rate bounds have a better asymptotic dependence on $n$ than the slow-rate PAC-Bayesian and single-draw bounds previously reported in *(Hellström and Durisi
comments.explanation: The claim that sublinear scaling guarantees a better asymptotic dependence than slow-rate bounds is mathematically imprecise. Slow-rate bounds typically exhibit an O(1/\sqrt{n}) dependence. If an information measure scales sublinearly but super-linearly to \sqrt{n} (for example, as n^{0.75}), dividing by n yields O(n^{-0.25}), which decays strictly slower than the slow-rate O(n^{-0.5}) bound. To ensure the proposed bounds actually achieve a faster rate than the slow-rate counterparts, the information measures must scale as o(\sqrt{n}). Please revise the conclusion to specify this stricter condition.

comments.title: Conceptual mismatch in posterior distribution intractability argument
comments.quote: nown. Furthermore, $P_{W|\bar{\bm{Z}}}$ is also difficult to compute, since marginalizing $P_{\bm{S}}P_{W|\bar{\bm{Z}}\bm{S}}$ over $\bm{S}$ involves performing training $2^{n}$ times. He
comments.explanation: Assuming \bar{\bm{Z}} is a typo for \widetilde{\bm{Z}}, the text claims that P_{W|\widetilde{\bm{Z}}} is intractable because computing it requires marginalizing over \bm{S} and simulating training 2^n times. However, in the random-subset setting, the learning algorithm only observes the training subset \bm{Z}(\bm{S}), not the full dataset \widetilde{\bm{Z}}. Consequently, P_{W|\widetilde{\bm{Z}}} does not represent the actual posterior distribution of the trained hypothesis; it is a hypothetical distribution conditioned on data the algorithm never sees. The true posterior is P_{W|\bm{Z}(\bm{S})}, which is tractable by definition. If P_{W|\widetilde{\bm{Z}}} is intended as an auxiliary distribution, the justification for its intractability is invalid because auxiliary distributions are chosen by the analyst and do not require running the training procedure. Clarify this distinction.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of CMI decomposition in Corollary 2
comments.quote: As illustrated in Corollary 2 below, the bound on the average population loss in (11) can be tightened by replacing the CMI with a sum of samplewise CMIs.
comments.explanation: The text claims that replacing the conditional mutual information I(W;\bm{S}|\widetilde{\bm{Z}}) with a sum of samplewise CMIs \sum_{i=1}^n I(W;S_i|\widetilde{\bm{Z}}) tightens the bound. However, by the chain rule for mutual information and the independence of the subset indicators S_i, we have the exact equality I(W;\bm{S}|\widetilde{\bm{Z}}) = \sum_{i=1}^n I(W;S_i|\widetilde{\bm{Z}}). This is not a theoretical tightening but an exact decomposition. Replace 'tightened by replacing' with 'equivalently decomposed into' to accurately reflect the mathematical relationship.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for the information measure
comments.quote: ed. Throughout the paper, we will, with an abuse of terminology, refer to bounds with an $n$-dependence of the form $\text{IM}(P_{W\bm{Z}})/n$ as fast-rate bounds.
comments.explanation: The notation P_{W\bm{Z}} refers to the joint distribution of the hypothesis and the training set \bm{Z}. However, the preceding sentences explicitly introduce the random-subset setting with a supersample \widetilde{\bm{Z}} and a selection vector \bm{S}, where the relevant information measure is I(W;\bm{S}|\widetilde{\bm{Z}}). Using P_{W\bm{Z}} here contradicts the notation established for the random-subset framework. Rewrite this to use \text{IM}(P_{W\bm{S}\widetilde{\bm{Z}}}) or explicitly state that \text{IM} denotes the conditional mutual information I(W;\bm{S}|\widetilde{\bm{Z}}) to maintain consistency.

comments.title: Clarify theoretical scope versus experimental instantiation
comments.quote: The single-draw bounds can be applied to deterministic NNs trained through SGD but with Gaussian noise added to the final weights, whereas the PAC-Bayesian bounds apply only to randomized neural networks, whose weights are drawn from a Gaussian distribution each time the network is used.
comments.explanation: The current phrasing conflates the general theoretical applicability of single-draw and PAC-Bayes bounds with a specific experimental implementation. Single-draw bounds apply to deterministic algorithms where a posterior is fixed after training, with the guarantee holding for a single draw from that posterior. Adding Gaussian noise to the final weights is merely one way to instantiate randomized inference for experiments, not a defining characteristic of the single-draw setting. Similarly, PAC-Bayes bounds apply to any randomized algorithm, not exclusively Gaussian ones. Rewrite the sentence to clarify that these distinctions describe the specific Gaussian setups used in the experiments, rather than the general theoretical scope of the bounds.

comments.title: Missing loss range assumption for fast-rate constants
comments.quote: y optimized). This difference explains why Theorem 1 allows for the derivation of fast-rate bounds, whereas *(Hellström and Durisi, 2020b, Thm. 4)* unavoidably leads to slow-rate bounds.
comments.explanation: The claim that Theorem 1 enables fast-rate bounds relies on the linear dependence on the population loss in the exponent. The specific constant factor 3 (and 2) in equation (10) arises from optimizing a Bernstein-type inequality, which requires explicit assumptions about the loss function's range (e.g., bounded in [0,1]) and variance. The paper states the derivation is in Appendix A.2 but does not specify these conditions in the main text. Add a sentence after equation (10) specifying the assumed range of the loss function and the variance condition used to derive the factor 3, or explicitly cite the lemma in Appendix A.2 that establishes these constants.

comments.title: Typographical error in Radon-Nikodym denominator
comments.quote: \log\frac{\mathrm{d}P_{W\widetilde{\bm{Z}}\bm{S}}}{\mathrm{d}Q_{W|\widetilde{\bm{Z}}}\widetilde{P_{\widetilde{\bm{Z}}\bm{S}}}}
comments.explanation: The denominator in the Radon-Nikodym derivative in equation (13) contains a spurious tilde over the probability measure P, written as \widetilde{P_{\widetilde{\bm{Z}}\bm{S}}}. This is a typographical error, as the corresponding change-of-measure term in Theorem 1 and equation (10) is correctly written as P_{\tilde{\bm{Z}}\bm{S}}. Remove the spurious tilde over P in the denominator of (13) to match the density ratio definition in Theorem 1.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper extends the random-subset information-theoretic framework to derive fast-rate (O(1/n)) PAC-Bayesian and single-draw test loss bounds for neural networks. The main theoretical contribution is an exponential inequality (Theorem 1) that yields bounds with linear dependence on the information measure, in contrast to the square-root dependence of prior slow-rate bounds. The experimental section evaluates these bounds on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST with fully-connected and convolutional architectures, showing that the fast-rate bounds are nonvacuous and competitive with state-of-the-art PAC-Bayesian bounds from Dziugaite et al. (2020).

The paper makes a clear theoretical contribution by providing fast-rate tail bounds in the random-subset setting, which were previously only available for average losses. The experimental validation is appropriate for the paper's scope and demonstrates that the bounds are practically meaningful. However, several issues regarding the proof of Theorem 1, the handling of the data-dependent prior, and the interpretation of the experimental results need to be addressed before the paper can be accepted.

Overview issues:

- Proof of Theorem 1 appears to have a gap in the change-of-measure argument: The proof of Theorem 1 in Appendix A.2 uses a change-of-measure argument that replaces the true joint distribution P_{W\tilde{Z}S} with Q_{W|\tilde{Z}} P_{\tilde{Z}} P_S. The critical step is the inequality E_{P_{W\tilde{Z}S}}[exp(f(W,\tilde{Z},S) - log(dP_{W\tilde{Z}S}/d(Q_{W|\tilde{Z}} P_{\tilde{Z}} P_S)))] ≤ 1, which requires that the Radon-Nikodym derivative is well-defined and that the exponential of the negative log-derivative integrates to 1 under the dominating measure. However, the paper does not verify that P_{W\tilde{Z}S} is absolutely continuous with respect to Q_{W|\tilde{Z}} P_{\tilde{Z}} P_S — it only states this as an assumption. More importantly, the function f(W,\tilde{Z},S) = (n/3)L_{Z(\bar{S})}(W) - (2n/3)L_{Z(S)}(W) is not bounded, so standard Donsker-Varadhan variational formula cannot be applied directly. The proof needs to either show that the exponential moment is finite or restrict to bounded losses. Without this, the inequality (10) is not justified.

- The data-dependent prior Q_{W|\tilde{Z}} violates the independence assumption required for the bounds: In Corollary 1, the bounds (12) and (13) require that the conditional prior Q_{W|\tilde{Z}} is independent of S given \tilde{Z}. However, the experimental procedure selects Q_{W|\tilde{Z}} by training networks on subsets of \tilde{Z} that are constructed using the same S-dependent selection mechanism. Specifically, the prior mean μ₂ is obtained by averaging weights from networks trained on subsets of \tilde{Z} that are formed by taking the first n, last n, and linearly spaced sequences — but these subsets are not independent of S because S determines which samples are used for training. The paper claims that μ₂ is independent of S because it is computed over the entire \tilde{Z}, but the subsets used to train the prior networks are themselves functions of the ordering of \tilde{Z}, which is randomized by the choice of S. This creates a subtle dependence that invalidates the theoretical guarantee that the bounds hold with probability at least 1-δ over P_{\tilde{Z}S}.

- The bound evaluation procedure does not properly account for multiple comparisons in the union bound: The paper states that a union bound is used over the 27 candidate values for σ₂, resulting in bounds that hold with probability at least 95%. However, the union bound is applied only to the optimization over σ₂, not to the multiple experimental runs (10 instances of S) or to the selection of the threshold used to determine σ₁ and σ₂. The paper reports mean bounds over 10 runs of S, but it is unclear whether the confidence guarantee applies to each individual run or to the reported mean. Furthermore, the selection of the threshold (0.05 for MNIST, 0.10 for Fashion-MNIST) is not accounted for in the union bound, which could lead to an optimistic bias in the reported bounds. The authors should provide a clear statement of what the 95% confidence guarantee covers and how the multiple data-dependent choices are handled.

- The connection between the theoretical fast-rate bounds and the experimental results is not fully established: The paper claims that the fast-rate bounds (12) and (13) have a 1/n dependence, but the experiments use a fixed n (the size of the training set) and do not vary n to demonstrate the claimed rate. Figure 1 shows bounds as a function of training epochs, not sample size. The experiments in Appendix D that vary n are not discussed in the main text and do not clearly show the 1/n scaling because the information measures themselves change with n. To substantiate the claim of fast rates, the paper should either provide a theoretical analysis of how the information measures scale with n for the specific architectures and training procedures used, or include experiments that demonstrate the n-dependence of the bounds while controlling for the information measure growth.

- The paper does not adequately address the computational cost and practical feasibility of the bounds: The experimental procedure requires training 11 networks (1 for the posterior, 10 for the prior) for each evaluation of the bounds, plus additional networks for the threshold selection procedure. For the LeNet-5 architecture, this is computationally manageable, but for larger modern architectures (e.g., ResNet, Transformer), this approach would be prohibitively expensive. The paper should discuss the scalability of the method and acknowledge that the current experimental validation is limited to relatively small networks. Additionally, the procedure for selecting σ₁ and σ₂ by checking the difference in training loss between the mean network and 5 sampled networks is ad hoc and not theoretically justified — the paper should provide a principled method for choosing these variances.

- The comparison with Dziugaite et al. (2020) is incomplete and potentially misleading: The paper states that the bounds essentially match those from Dziugaite et al. (2020), but the comparison is based on bounds from Dziugaite et al.'s Figure 4, which are not their tightest results. Dziugaite et al.'s Figure 5 shows significantly tighter bounds (around 0.08 for MNIST LeNet-5) when the bound is used as a regularizer during training. The paper acknowledges this but dismisses it by saying such optimization is not feasible in the random-subset setting. However, this is a significant limitation of the proposed approach — the bounds are not tight enough to be used as training objectives, which is one of the main applications of PAC-Bayesian bounds. The paper should either explain why direct optimization is impossible in principle, or acknowledge this as a limitation that future work should address.

- No demonstration of fast-rate scaling with n: The paper claims fast-rate (1/n) bounds but never shows this scaling empirically. Figure 1 plots bounds against training epochs at a fixed n, and the Appendix D experiments that vary n are not analyzed to extract the rate. To substantiate the central claim, the authors should add a plot of the bound value versus n (e.g., n = 500, 1000, 2000, 5000, 10000) on a log-log scale for LeNet-5 on MNIST, with the slow-rate and fast-rate bounds overlaid. They should also discuss how the information measures scale with n in practice, since the 1/n rate only holds if those measures grow sublinearly.

- Missing worked example for a simple parametric model: The paper derives general bounds but never computes them for a simple, fully-specified model where the true population loss is known. A natural example would be a Gaussian location model: let Z ~ N(θ, 1), W be the sample mean of the training set, and ℓ(w, z) = 1{|w - z| > ε}. For this model, one can compute the CMI, the KL divergences, and the bounds in closed form, and compare them to the true population loss. This would let readers calibrate how tight the bounds are in a setting with no approximation error, and would clarify the role of the multiplicative constants.

- No guidance on choosing the posterior variance σ₁: The experimental procedure for selecting σ₁ is ad hoc: it picks the largest σ₁ such that the training loss of the mean network and the average of 5 sampled networks differ by less than a threshold. This choice is not theoretically justified and the threshold (0.05 for MNIST, 0.10 for Fashion-MNIST) is arbitrary. The paper should either provide a principled criterion (e.g., based on the bound itself) or discuss how sensitive the bounds are to this choice. Without this, the experimental results are not reproducible and the method cannot be applied by other researchers.

- Comparison with Dziugaite et al. omits their best results: The paper compares its bounds to Dziugaite et al.'s Figure 4, but Figure 5 of that paper reports tighter bounds (≈0.08 for MNIST LeNet-5) obtained by using the bound as a regularizer. The paper dismisses this by saying such optimization is not feasible in the random-subset setting, but does not explain why. This is a significant limitation: one of the main uses of PAC-Bayesian bounds is as training objectives. The authors should either prove that direct optimization is impossible in their framework, or acknowledge this as a limitation and suggest how future work might address it.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Fix the proof of Theorem 1 by either (a) showing that the exponential moment is finite under the bounded loss assumption, or (b) adding an explicit boundedness condition on the loss function that ensures the Donsker-Varadhan inequality can be applied.

- Clarify the independence of the prior Q_{W|\tilde{Z}} from S, and either modify the experimental procedure to ensure this independence or provide a theoretical justification that the dependence is negligible.

- Provide a clear and complete description of the union bound procedure, including all sources of multiple comparisons (threshold selection, σ₂ candidates, multiple runs of S), and report bounds that are valid with the stated confidence level.

- Include experiments that vary the sample size n and demonstrate the 1/n scaling of the bounds, along with a theoretical analysis of how the information measures grow with n for the specific architectures used.

- Add a discussion of the computational cost and scalability of the method, and provide a principled procedure for selecting the posterior and prior variances that does not rely on ad hoc threshold checks.

comments.title: Absolute continuity assumption needs justification
comments.quote: assume that the joint distribution $P_{W\tilde{\bm{Z}}\bm{S}}=P_{W|\tilde{\bm{Z}}\bm{S}}P_{\tilde{\bm{Z}}}P_{\bm{S}}$ is absolutely continuous with respect to $Q_{W|\tilde{\bm{Z}}}P_{\tilde{\bm{Z}}}P_{\bm{S}}$ for some conditional prior $Q_{W|\tilde{\bm{Z}}}$.
comments.explanation: The paper states absolute continuity as an assumption but never discusses when it holds. In practice, if the posterior assigns positive probability to regions where the prior assigns zero probability, the Radon-Nikodym derivative is undefined and inequality (10) fails. The authors should provide sufficient conditions (e.g., the prior has full support on the weight space) or restrict the class of priors and posteriors accordingly. Without this, the theoretical foundation of the bounds is incomplete.

comments.title: Prior construction depends on S through ordering
comments.quote: To select the prior $Q_{W|\widetilde{\bm{Z}}}$, we proceed as follows. We form $10$ subsets of $\widetilde{\bm{Z}}$, each of size $n$. The first subset contains the first $n$ samples in $\widetilde{\bm{Z}}$, the last contains the last $n$ samples in $\widetilde{\bm{Z}}$, and the remaining subsets contain the linearly spaced sequences in between.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that (μ₂, σ₂) are independent of S, but the construction of the 10 subsets uses the ordering of samples in ̃Z. The text later states that 'since we pick the training set uniformly at random from ̃Z, we implicitly randomize over the ordering of the elements of ̃Z.' This means the ordering is a function of S, so the subsets 'first n samples', 'last n samples', and 'linearly spaced sequences' depend on S through the ordering. Consequently, μ₂ depends on S, violating the independence assumption required for the bounds. The claim that the training loss for selecting σ̃₂ is evaluated over the entire ̃Z does not address the S-dependence of μ₂. The authors should either rewrite the prior construction to use S-independent subsets (e.g., fixed indices not tied to ordering) or provide a theoretical justification that the ordering randomization does not create dependence.

comments.title: Threshold selection for σ₁ and σ₂ lacks justification
comments.quote: Unless otherwise specified, we use a threshold of $0.05$ for MNIST and $0.10$ for Fashion-MNIST. Note that this procedure is performed for a fixed $\bm{Z}(\bm{S})$. Consequently, $\sigma_{1}$ depends on $\widetilde{\bm{Z}}$ and $\bm{S}$.
comments.explanation: The threshold values (0.05 for MNIST, 0.10 for Fashion-MNIST) are presented without justification. Since σ₁ directly controls the KL divergence term in the PAC-Bayesian bound, an arbitrary threshold can produce bounds that are either too loose (if σ₁ is too small) or potentially invalid (if σ₁ is too large, violating the condition that the training loss difference is small). The paper should provide a principled criterion for selecting the threshold, such as minimizing the resulting bound or ensuring that the bound's assumptions are satisfied with high probability. Without this, the experimental results are not fully reproducible.

comments.title: Monte Carlo estimate uses only 5 samples
comments.quote: the absolute value of the difference between the training loss of the network with weights $\bm{\mu}_{1}$ and the empirical average of the training loss achieved by $5$ NNs with weights randomly drawn from $\mathcal{N}(W\mid\bm{\mu}_{1},\sigma_{1}^{2}\mathbf{I}_{d})$ is less than some specified threshold.
comments.explanation: The procedure uses only 5 Monte Carlo samples to estimate the average training loss of the posterior. With such a small sample size, the estimate has high variance, and the condition that the difference is below a threshold may be satisfied by chance even when the true average loss is far from the loss at μ₁. This could lead to an overestimated σ₁ and consequently an overly large KL divergence term, potentially invalidating the bound's tightness. The paper should justify why 5 samples are sufficient, e.g., by showing that the variance of the estimate is small or by using a larger number of samples.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of Steinke and Zakynthinou bound
comments.quote: Notably, *Steinke and Zakynthinou (2020, Thm. 2(3))* derive a population loss bound whose dependence on $n$ is $I(W;\bm{S}|\tilde{\bm{Z}})/n$. The price for this improved dependence is that the training loss that is added to the $n$-dependent term is multiplied by a constant larger than $1$.
comments.explanation: Theorem 2(3) in Steinke and Zakynthinou (2020) gives E[L_{Z(\bar{S})}(W)] ≤ E[L_{Z(S)}(W)] + I(W;S|̃Z)/n, where the training loss term has coefficient 1, not a constant larger than 1. The paper's claim that the training loss is multiplied by a constant larger than 1 is inaccurate for this specific theorem. The authors may be conflating with other bounds (e.g., Theorem 2(2) or later results), but as written it misrepresents the result. The sentence should be rewritten to state that the training loss appears with coefficient 1.

comments.title: Threshold condition may be impossible to satisfy for large σ₁
comments.quote: The standard deviation $\sigma_{1}$ is chosen as the largest real number, determined to some finite precision (see Appendix C.2), for which the absolute value of the difference between the training loss of the network with weights $\bm{\mu}_{1}$ and the empirical average of the training loss achieved by $5$ NNs with weights randomly drawn from $\mathcal{N}(W\mid\bm{\mu}_{1},\sigma_{1}^{2}\mathbf{I}_{d})$ is less than some specified threshold.
comments.explanation: The procedure searches for the largest σ₁ such that the loss difference is below a threshold. However, for very large σ₁, the randomly sampled weights will be far from μ₁, and the training loss may become arbitrarily large (e.g., due to activation saturation or numerical overflow), making it impossible to satisfy the threshold condition. The paper does not discuss how to handle cases where no σ₁ satisfies the condition, or where the condition is satisfied only for very small σ₁, leading to trivial bounds. The authors should specify a fallback procedure, such as using a fixed small σ₁ or reporting that the bound is vacuous in such cases.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for information measure
comments.quote: Throughout the paper, we will, with an abuse of terminology, refer to bounds with an $n$-dependence of the form $\text{IM}(P_{W\bm{Z}})/n$ as fast-rate bounds.
comments.explanation: The notation IM(P_{WZ}) is ambiguous because the paper's own bounds involve conditional mutual information I(W;S|̃Z), not a joint distribution of W and Z. The paper defines IM(P_{WZ}) earlier as an information measure like mutual information or maximal leakage, but in the random-subset setting the relevant quantity is I(W;S|̃Z). The inconsistency could confuse readers. The paper should clarify that in the random-subset setting, the information measure is replaced by I(W;S|̃Z).

comments.title: KL divergence formula assumes Gaussian prior and posterior
comments.explanation: The KL divergence formula (15) is correct for two multivariate Gaussians with diagonal covariance matrices σ₁²I_d and σ₂²I_d. However, the derivation assumes that the posterior P_{W|̃Z S} is exactly N(μ₁, σ₁²I_d) and the prior Q_{W|̃Z} is exactly N(μ₂, σ₂²I_d). The paper does not justify that the posterior is Gaussian; it is only stated that the posterior is chosen as a Gaussian. The bounds (8), (9), (12), (13) are general and do not require Gaussianity, but the evaluation of KL divergences via (15) is valid only if both distributions are exactly Gaussian. The paper should explicitly state that the posterior is taken to be Gaussian and that this choice is consistent with the bounds.
